---
title: "20 · Robbins-Monro: Solving g(w)=0 from Noisy Samples"
subtitle: "A general iterative scheme that solves g(w)=0 when you can only sample noisy estimates of g."
lesson-id: "mfrl.robbins-monro"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.epsilon-greedy", "t5.sequences-series-convergence"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Before you start, you should be able to say *yes* to all three:

1. Can you read the iteration $w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k \, y_k$ and explain in
   words what each symbol does? — see [MFRL — 16, Mean Estimation from Samples](../16-mean-estimation-from-samples/index.qmd).
2. Do you know what it means for an infinite series $\sum_k a_k$ to *converge*
   versus *diverge*? — see [Tier 5, Lesson 14 — Sequences, Series, and Convergence](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/14-sequences-series-convergence/index.qmd).
3. Are you comfortable with the idea that a sample average estimates an
   expectation $\mathbb{E}[X]$? — same prerequisite.

If any answer is *no*, follow the link before continuing.
:::

## Touch

You want to find a number $w^\*$ where some hidden function $g$ vanishes:
$g(w^\*) = 0$. The catch: every time you ask for $g(w)$, a malicious assistant
adds noise before handing the value back. You only ever see
$y = g(w) + \text{noise}$.

Drag the slider. The blue dots are noisy samples of $g$ at the $w$ you pick;
the dashed grey line is the true (hidden) $g$.

```{ojs}
viewof wprobe = Inputs.range([-2, 4], {value: 0.5, step: 0.05, label: "Probe at w ="})
viewof noiseStd = Inputs.range([0, 1.5], {value: 0.6, step: 0.05, label: "Noise σ ="})
viewof rngSeed = Inputs.range([1, 100], {value: 7, step: 1, label: "Reseed:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  // Hidden true root: g(w) = w - 1.7 (so w* = 1.7)
  const wstar = 1.7;
  const trueG = w => w - wstar;

  // mulberry32 PRNG so the slider doesn't churn samples on tiny moves
  function mulberry32(a) {
    return function() {
      a |= 0; a = a + 0x6D2B79F5 | 0;
      let t = a;
      t = Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 15, t | 1);
      t ^= t + Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 7, t | 61);
      return ((t ^ t >>> 14) >>> 0) / 4294967296;
    };
  }
  const rand = mulberry32(rngSeed * 1000 + Math.floor(wprobe * 100));

  // Box-Muller normal noise
  const u1 = rand(), u2 = rand();
  const z = Math.sqrt(-2 * Math.log(u1 + 1e-12)) * Math.cos(2 * Math.PI * u2);
  const sample = trueG(wprobe) + noiseStd * z;

  // Curve points for the dashed truth
  const xs = d3.range(-2, 4.05, 0.05);
  const truth = xs.map(w => ({w, g: trueG(w)}));

  const Plot = await import("https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@observablehq/plot@0.6/+esm");
  return Plot.plot({
    width: 540, height: 280,
    x: {label: "w", domain: [-2, 4]},
    y: {label: "g(w) (and noisy sample)", domain: [-4, 4]},
    marks: [
      Plot.ruleY([0]),
      Plot.line(truth, {x: "w", y: "g", stroke: "#999", strokeDasharray: "4 3"}),
      Plot.dot([{w: wprobe, y: sample}], {x: "w", y: "y", fill: "steelblue", r: 6}),
      Plot.text([{w: wprobe, y: sample}], {x: "w", y: "y", text: d => `y = ${d.y.toFixed(2)}`, dy: -14, fontSize: 11})
    ]
  });
}
```

The hidden truth is $g(w) = w - 1.7$, so the answer you are after is
$w^\* = 1.7$. But you can never see the dashed line — only the noisy blue dot.

Your only handle on the world is the noisy sample. *How would you build a
sequence of probes that homes in on $w^\*$?*

## Wonder

Sit with these for a moment.

1. If a probe at some $w_k$ returns $y_k > 0$, what does that *suggest* about
   whether $w_k$ is to the left or right of $w^\*$? Does the noise change your
   answer?
2. Suppose you nudge $w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha \, y_k$ with a fixed step $\alpha$.
   What happens once you arrive near $w^\*$ but the noise keeps wobbling $y_k$?
3. What if instead $\alpha$ shrank with $k$ — say $\alpha_k = 1/k$? Could the
   sequence still *make progress*, or would it freeze too soon?
4. Is there an in-between: shrink fast enough to kill the noise but slowly
   enough to keep moving?

## Discover

Five short problems. Each one cracks open one piece of the algorithm.

### Problem 1 — the goal

You want $w^\*$ with $g(w^\*) = 0$, but every query returns
$y = g(w) + \eta$ for some zero-mean noise $\eta$. Why can't you simply solve
$y = 0$ by setting $g(w) = 0$ and inverting?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You don't have $g$. You have *one* noisy number per query.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
You don't know $g$ as a formula — only as a black box you can poke. A single
sample carries noise, so even at the right $w$ you'd see $y \neq 0$. You need
*many* samples and a way to average their information.
</details>

### Problem 2 — sign tells direction

Suppose $g$ is increasing. If $y_k = g(w_k) + \eta_k$ comes back positive (on
average), where is $w^\*$ relative to $w_k$? What if $y_k$ is negative?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Increasing $g$ means $g(w) > 0$ when $w$ is *above* the root.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For increasing $g$: $y > 0 \Rightarrow w > w^\*$ (move *down*); $y < 0 \Rightarrow w < w^\*$ (move *up*). So the natural update is
$$w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k \, y_k.$$
Subtracting a positive $y$ moves $w$ down — exactly the direction of $w^\*$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — what a constant $\alpha$ buys you

Try the update $w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha \, y_k$ with $\alpha = 0.1$ on
$g(w) = w - 1.7$ and noise $\eta_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$. Will $w_k$
*converge* to $1.7$, or will it bounce around it forever?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
At $w = 1.7$, the update becomes $w_{k+1} = 1.7 - 0.1 \cdot \eta_k$. What does
that look like in the long run?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Compute the variance of $w_{k+1}$ given $w_k = w^\*$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
A constant $\alpha$ can only *contract* the bias; it cannot kill the noise.
Even sitting on $w^\*$, the next step jumps by $-\alpha \eta_k$, which has
variance $\alpha^2 \sigma^2$. So $w_k$ wanders forever in a cloud of radius
$\sim \alpha \sigma$. **Constant step ⇒ bouncing, not convergence.**
</details>

### Problem 4 — a shrinking $\alpha_k$

Try $\alpha_k = 1/k$. At step 1 the step is huge; by step 100 it's $0.01$.
Two opposing forces appear. Name them.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
One force lets the iteration *travel*. The other lets it *settle*.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
*Travel:* the steps must add up to enough total motion to reach $w^\*$ even
from a far-away start. That requires $\sum_k \alpha_k = \infty$.
*Settle:* the noise injected at each step, $\alpha_k \eta_k$, must sum to
something finite — otherwise it never stops jiggling. That requires
$\sum_k \alpha_k^2 < \infty$.

The choice $\alpha_k = 1/k$ satisfies both: $\sum 1/k = \infty$ but
$\sum 1/k^2 = \pi^2/6 < \infty$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — apply it to the mean

Take $g(w) = w - \mathbb{E}[X]$, where $X$ is some random variable you can
sample. Then $g(w^\*) = 0$ has solution $w^\* = \mathbb{E}[X]$. The Robbins-Monro
update with $\alpha_k = 1/k$ becomes:
$$w_{k+1} = w_k - \frac{1}{k}(w_k - X_k) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right) w_k + \frac{1}{k} X_k.$$

Why is the right-hand side just the running mean of $X_1, \ldots, X_k$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Expand $w_{k+1}$ recursively starting from $w_1 = X_1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Unrolling: $w_{k+1} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^k X_i$. The classical sample mean
*is* a Robbins-Monro iteration in disguise. So the famous law-of-large-numbers
estimator is the simplest member of an enormous family.
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
Stochastic root-finding works when the step sequence does two contradictory
things at once: $\sum_k \alpha_k = \infty$ (still moving) and
$\sum_k \alpha_k^2 < \infty$ (eventually still). The harmonic schedule
$\alpha_k = 1/k$ is the canonical example.
:::

::: {.fun-fact}
Herbert **Robbins** and Sutton **Monro** were a Columbia statistician and a
graduate student when they published their 1951 paper in the *Annals of
Mathematical Statistics*. The seven-page note received almost no attention for
twenty years; today it is one of the most-cited statistics papers of the
twentieth century, because every machine-learning optimiser is a descendant.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Robbins-Monro algorithm.** Given a black-box function $g: \mathbb{R} \to
\mathbb{R}$ with unknown root $w^\*$, accessed only through noisy samples
$y_k = g(w_k) + \eta_k$ with $\mathbb{E}[\eta_k \mid w_k] = 0$, the iteration
$$
w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k \, y_k
$$
converges almost surely to $w^\*$ provided

- **(R1) Step sums:** $\sum_{k=1}^\infty \alpha_k = \infty$ and
  $\sum_{k=1}^\infty \alpha_k^2 < \infty$.
- **(R2) $g$ regularity:** $g$ is monotonic and has bounded slope around
  $w^\*$.
- **(R3) Noise bound:** $\mathbb{E}[\eta_k^2 \mid w_k]$ is bounded.

:::

A short verse to memorise the step conditions, in the spirit of Bhāskara's
*Līlāvatī*:

> *Sum of steps must be unbounded — to walk all the way home.*
> *Sum of squares must be finite — to silence the noise.*
> *Both at once: the harmonic step $\alpha_k = 1/k$.*

## Apply

### Worked example — running-mean estimation by RM

> A sensor returns noisy readings $X_1, X_2, X_3, \ldots$ of a fixed but
> unknown temperature $\mu$. Estimate $\mu$ on the fly using Robbins-Monro
> with $\alpha_k = 1/k$. Read out the estimate after 4 samples
> $X_1 = 19, X_2 = 21, X_3 = 22, X_4 = 18$.

A competent solver thinks like this:

1. Set up: I want $\mu = \mathbb{E}[X]$, so let $g(w) = w - \mu$. The noisy
   sample of $g(w)$ is $w - X_k$. The RM update becomes
   $w_{k+1} = w_k - \frac{1}{k}(w_k - X_k)$.
2. Initialise $w_1 = X_1 = 19$.
3. Step $k=1$: $w_2 = 19 - 1 \cdot (19 - 21) = 19 + 2 = 21$. (Wait — for the
   simple unrolling identity I want to start the index so that $w_{k+1}$
   uses $\alpha_k = 1/k$ with $w_1 = X_1$ as the seed; that's the convention
   used in the Discover sequence above.)
4. Re-check by the running-mean formula: $w_2 = (X_1+X_2)/2 = 20$.
5. So the careful update from $w_1=19, X_2=21$ with $\alpha_1 = 1/2$ is
   $w_2 = 19 - \tfrac{1}{2}(19 - 21) = 20$. **Lesson: the index where you
   *start* counting $k$ matters. Pick a convention and stick with it.**
6. Continuing with $\alpha_k = 1/(k+1)$: $w_3 = w_2 - \tfrac{1}{3}(w_2 - X_3) = 20 - \tfrac{1}{3}(20 - 22) = 20.667$. $w_4 = 20.667 - \tfrac{1}{4}(20.667 - 18) = 20$.

**Estimate after 4 samples: $\hat\mu = 20.0$.**

A sanity check: $(19 + 21 + 22 + 18)/4 = 20$. The iteration matches the sample
mean exactly, as it must.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** For each schedule, decide whether (R1) holds:
$\alpha_k = 1$, $\alpha_k = 1/k$, $\alpha_k = 1/k^{0.6}$, $\alpha_k = 1/k^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use the *p-series* test: $\sum 1/k^p$ converges iff $p > 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- $\alpha_k = 1$: $\sum \alpha_k = \infty$ ✓ but $\sum \alpha_k^2 = \infty$ ✗.
- $\alpha_k = 1/k$: $\sum 1/k = \infty$ ✓ and $\sum 1/k^2 < \infty$ ✓. **Both hold.**
- $\alpha_k = 1/k^{0.6}$: $\sum 1/k^{0.6} = \infty$ ✓; $\sum 1/k^{1.2} < \infty$ ✓. **Both hold.**
- $\alpha_k = 1/k^2$: $\sum 1/k^2 < \infty$ ✗ (steps die too fast).
</details>

**W2.** A friend proposes $\alpha_k = 0.1$ (constant). Will RM converge?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
No. $\sum \alpha_k^2 = \infty$, so (R1) fails. The iterate bounces around
$w^\*$ in a cloud of radius $\sim \alpha \sigma$ forever. Constant step is
common in practice for *non-stationary* targets, where you *want* to keep
tracking — but it does not satisfy the RM convergence theorem.
</details>

**W3.** State the Robbins-Monro update for finding the root of $g(w) = w^3 - 8$
(noise-free). Run two steps from $w_1 = 1$ with $\alpha_k = 1/k$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$w_2 = 1 - 1\cdot(1 - 8) = 1 - (-7) = 8$. $w_3 = 8 - \tfrac12 (8^3 - 8) = 8 - \tfrac12 \cdot 504 = -244$. **The iteration explodes** — because $g$ is steep
and $\alpha_1$ is too large. Lesson: RM theory needs $g$'s slope to be
bounded; in practice you also clip steps.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** You sample $X_k$ from a Bernoulli(0.3) distribution (so $X_k \in \{0,1\}$,
$\mathbb{E}[X] = 0.3$). Run RM for the mean with $w_1 = 0.5$ and
$\alpha_k = 1/(k+1)$ on the sequence $X_1, X_2, X_3 = 0, 1, 0$. Compute $w_2,
w_3, w_4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$g(w) = w - X$; update $w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k(w_k - X_k)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$w_2 = 0.5 - \tfrac12(0.5 - 0) = 0.25$.
$w_3 = 0.25 - \tfrac13(0.25 - 1) = 0.5$.
$w_4 = 0.5 - \tfrac14(0.5 - 0) = 0.375$.
The estimate drifts toward $0.3$ as more samples come in.
</details>

**P2.** Suppose $g(w) = w - 5$ and the noise $\eta_k$ is *biased*: $\mathbb{E}[\eta_k] = 1$ instead of $0$. What does RM converge to (if anything)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
At convergence, $\mathbb{E}[y_k] = 0$. Solve for $w^\*$ when the noise has a
non-zero mean.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The condition becomes $g(w^\*) + 1 = 0$, i.e., $w^\* - 5 + 1 = 0$, so
$w^\* = 4$. **Bias in the noise produces a bias in the answer.** This is why
ε-greedy targets in TD must be *unbiased* estimators of the Bellman target —
otherwise the converged value will be wrong.
</details>

**P3.** Use RM to estimate $\sqrt{2}$ as the root of $g(w) = w^2 - 2$, starting
from $w_1 = 1.5$ with $\alpha_k = 0.3 / k$. Compute $w_2, w_3, w_4$
(noise-free).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The noise-free RM iteration becomes $w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k (w_k^2 - 2)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$w_2 = 1.5 - 0.3 \cdot (2.25 - 2) = 1.5 - 0.075 = 1.425$.
$w_3 = 1.425 - 0.15 \cdot (1.425^2 - 2) = 1.425 - 0.15 \cdot 0.0306 = 1.4204$.
$w_4 = 1.4204 - 0.1 \cdot (1.4204^2 - 2) = 1.4204 - 0.0175 = 1.4179$.
The exact answer is $1.41421\ldots$. RM is converging — slowly, because we
chose a small constant in front of $\alpha_k$ to keep things stable.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** *Why must the step sums diverge?* Suppose $\sum_k \alpha_k = C < \infty$.
Show that the RM iterate cannot, in general, reach a target that is more than
$C \cdot \sup_k |y_k|$ away from $w_1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Bound $|w_{k+1} - w_1|$ by a sum of step sizes times sample sizes.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Triangle inequality on the recursion.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
By the triangle inequality,
$|w_{n+1} - w_1| \le \sum_{k=1}^n \alpha_k |y_k| \le \sup_k |y_k| \cdot \sum_k \alpha_k \le C \sup_k |y_k|$.
So the iterate is *trapped* within a ball of finite radius around the start.
If $w^\*$ is outside that ball, you can never reach it. **Divergent step sums
are the price of being able to travel any finite distance.**
</details>

**S2.** RM solves $g(w) = 0$. Show that minimising $f$ via $g(w) = \nabla f(w)$
turns RM into [stochastic gradient descent](#sgd-preview). What does the noisy
sample $y_k$ become?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
What's the analogue of "noisy $g$" when $g$ is a gradient?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Pick any unbiased estimator $\hat\nabla_k$ of $\nabla f(w_k)$ — for instance,
the gradient on a single training example. Then $y_k = \hat\nabla_k$, and the
RM update becomes $w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k \hat\nabla_k$. This is **SGD**.
RM and SGD are the same algorithm with two different stories. We expand this
identification in **[Lesson 22 — SGD](../22-stochastic-gradient-descent/index.qmd)**.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Constant step size is fine in practice, so the conditions don't
   matter."** Constant $\alpha$ gives an iterate that *tracks* the target
   with bias-variance tradeoff $\sim \alpha \sigma$. That's useful for
   *non-stationary* problems (where the target is drifting) but it does not
   satisfy the convergence theorem.
2. **"$\alpha_k = 1/k^2$ should be even better than $1/k$ since it's
   smaller."** No — it dies too fast. The iterate freezes before reaching
   $w^\*$. Both conditions in (R1) matter; the *travel* condition fails for
   $\alpha_k = 1/k^2$.
3. **"RM needs an unbiased noise."** The theorem demands
   $\mathbb{E}[\eta_k \mid w_k] = 0$. If the noise is biased, RM converges
   to the wrong root (Working P2).
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **Robbins-Monro turns a noisy black-box into a root-finder.** One iteration:
   $w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k y_k$.
2. **Step sums must diverge; step-square sums must converge.** $\alpha_k = 1/k$
   is the canonical schedule.
3. **The sample mean is a special case of RM.** And so are SGD, TD learning,
   Sarsa, and Q-learning — every one of them is a root-finder in disguise.
   This is the central insight that powers the rest of this chapter and the
   next.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you explain to a friend, without notes, *why* both step conditions
   matter — what each one prevents?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 8 practice problems without peeking past
   *Hint 1*?
3. Can you spot the running mean as a Robbins-Monro iteration when you see
   $w_{k+1} = (1 - 1/k) w_k + (1/k) X_k$?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Lesson 21 — Dvoretzky's Theorem](../21-dvoretzky-convergence/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S1 — the triangle-inequality argument is the
  intuition for *every* convergence proof in this track.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* Problem 4 (the two forces) and the worked
  example. The bias/variance dance has not landed yet.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[MFRL — 19, ε-Greedy](../19-epsilon-greedy/index.qmd)** — the ε-greedy
  policy gives the sampling distribution we will eventually use to drive RM
  for value learning.
- **[Tier 5, Lesson 14 — Sequences, Series, and Convergence](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/14-sequences-series-convergence/index.qmd)** — the p-series test we
  used in W1, and the general idea of divergent versus convergent infinite
  sums.
- **[MFRL — 16, Mean Estimation from Samples](../16-mean-estimation-from-samples/index.qmd)** — the running-mean update is the simplest RM iteration.

### What this unlocks

- **[MFRL — 21, Dvoretzky's Convergence Theorem](../21-dvoretzky-convergence/index.qmd)** — the generalisation that subsumes RM and applies to TD,
  Q-learning, and SGD.
- **[MFRL — 22, SGD](../22-stochastic-gradient-descent/index.qmd)** — RM applied
  to $g(w) = \nabla f(w) = 0$.
- **[MFRL — 23, TD(0)](../23-td-state-values/index.qmd)** — RM applied to the
  Bellman equation.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Every iterative optimiser used to train modern neural networks —
SGD, momentum, Adam, RMSProp — is a Robbins-Monro descendant. The
[learning rate](/glossary.qmd#learning-rate) schedule you choose is exactly
the $\alpha_k$ sequence; understanding (R1) is why warmup-then-decay schedules
work and constant learning rates do not converge.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Stochastic approximation underlies online learning, adaptive
control, recursive estimation in Kalman filters, and the convergence proofs
of every TD method. Almost any infinite-time learning theorem reduces, after
peeling, to "this is RM in disguise."
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning that this track
expands into 40 lessons.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Source:** Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement
  Learning* (Springer 2025), Chapter 6 §6.1. Cited freely; never transcribed.
- **Touchstones honoured:** Russian school problem-first sequencing
  (Discover before Name), Better Explained "Aha!" framing (the noisy
  black-box metaphor), Bhāskara verse-style mnemonic for the step
  conditions.
- **Self-study guarantees:** pre-flight ✓, multiple representations
  (widget, formula, English) ✓, graduated hints ✓, worked solutions ✓,
  common-confusions ✓, self-assessment ✓.
:::
